title: AIP Assist - Custom content source best practices - Palantir Docs

extractedEntities.name: AIP Assist
extractedEntities.description: AI-powered assistant within Foundry that uses retrieval augmented generation to answer user questions by retrieving relevant content from custom and public documentation sources.

extractedEntities.name: Retrieval Augmented Generation
extractedEntities.description: Underlying mechanism used by AIP Assist that divides content into focused paragraphs, retrieves the most pertinent paragraphs when a question is asked, and uses them alongside public documentation to formulate responses.

extractedEntities.name: Custom Content Source
extractedEntities.description: User-provided documentation content that powers AIP Assist responses, organized into discrete sections with headings and subheadings for optimal retrieval granularity.

extractedEntities.name: Content Structuring for RAG
extractedEntities.description: Best practice pattern of organizing content into discrete sections based on headings and subheadings, where each heading is dedicated to a single topic, ensuring high relevance and specificity for retrieval.

extractedEntities.name: RAG Paragraph Retrieval
extractedEntities.description: The process where RAG identifies and retrieves the paragraphs most pertinent to a question, with content granularity significantly impacting response quality.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform that hosts AIP Assist, public documentation, and custom content sources.

keyInsights: AIP Assist uses RAG to divide content into focused paragraphs and retrieve the most pertinent ones when a question is asked, making content granularity critical to response quality.
Best practice is to organize custom content into discrete sections with one topic per heading, avoiding long multi-topic paragraphs that dilute retrieval relevance.
Custom content must not overlap with Palantir public documentation; common terms should be clearly disambiguated within their context to avoid retrieval collisions.
Deprecated or overridden content should be removed or updated at its source rather than annotated with deprecation notices, which confuse the RAG retrieval mechanism.

title: Automate - Overview - Palantir Docs

extractedEntities.name: Automate
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for business automation that replaces Object Monitoring as the single entry point for all business automation, allowing users to define conditions checked continuously or on a schedule with effects that execute automatically when conditions are met.

extractedEntities.name: Condition
extractedEntities.description: A trigger definition in Automate that can be time-based (e.g., schedule), object data conditions built on the Ontology (e.g., when a new object matching criteria is added), or a combination of both.

extractedEntities.name: Effect
extractedEntities.description: An action that executes automatically in Automate when specified conditions are met, including submitting Foundry actions, triggering AIP Logic functions, and sending notifications with attachments.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology concept representing executable business operations that can be submitted as effects within Automate workflows.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity representing business objects whose data conditions can trigger Automate workflows.

extractedEntities.name: AIP Logic
extractedEntities.description: LLM-powered function development environment whose functions can be triggered as effects by Automate.

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: A collection of objects that can be watched by Automate for data alerting when specific criteria are met.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core platform that hosts the Automate application.

extractedEntities.name: Event-Driven Automation
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where Automate watches for object data changes and triggers effects when conditions are met, enabling real-time business process automation.

keyInsights: Automate is the fully backwards-compatible successor to Object Monitoring, serving as the single entry point for all business automation in Foundry.
Conditions can be time-based, object data-based (built on the Foundry Ontology), or a combination, enabling both scheduled and real-time event-driven automation.
Available effects include submitting Foundry actions, triggering AIP Logic functions, and sending platform/email notifications with attachments (e.g., PDFs from Notepad).
Use cases span scheduled reporting, data alerting on object set thresholds, workflow automation with remediation actions, and watched searches with aggregate criteria.
Automate can watch object explorations for new results or when aggregate criteria (e.g., maximum temperature across sensors) cross a threshold.

title: Workshop - AIP widgets - AIP Analyst - Palantir Docs

extractedEntities.name: Workshop

extractedEntities.name: Widget

keyInsights: AIP Analyst is a beta Workshop widget that embeds AI-powered analysis capabilities directly into operational modules.
The widget supports extensive configuration for data access control, tool availability, and user interface customization.
AIP Analyst must be enabled by a platform administrator through Control Panel application access settings.
It sits in the widget hierarchy between Audio Recorder and AIP Agent, indicating a family of AI-powered Workshop widgets.

title: Palantir's Secret Weapon: The LLM Synthesiser Secures Its AI Lead - Strategic Foresight

extractedEntities.name: LLM Synthesiser
extractedEntities.description: Specialized system that brings together multiple large language models into Palantir's Ontology Platform, cross-validates their responses against real-world data, and produces a reliable composite answer to reduce hallucination and increase accuracy.

extractedEntities.name: Multi-Model Cross-Validation
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where multiple LLMs each provide independent responses to a query, the system compares and rates them against real-world data in the Ontology Platform, and produces the best answer along with alternative viewpoints and model performance details.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Platform
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's powerful data organization and analysis platform that stores real-world data used to cross-validate LLM responses in the Synthesiser.

extractedEntities.name: Synthesiser Output
extractedEntities.description: The composite output of the LLM Synthesiser includes the best answer (most accurate and relevant), different points of view from other models, and performance details on which AIs were right or wrong with reasons.

extractedEntities.name: Healthcare Resource Allocation
extractedEntities.description: Example use case where the LLM Synthesiser helps a hospital manager decide which areas need medical supplies during a disease outbreak by cross-validating epidemiology, logistics, and demographics models against real data.

extractedEntities.name: Hallucination Reduction
extractedEntities.description: Strategy of using multiple LLMs and cross-validating against grounded data to reduce the risk of AI hallucination in critical decision-making scenarios.

keyInsights: The LLM Synthesiser pings multiple LLMs for independent responses, then compares, rates, and cross-validates them against real-world data in the Ontology Platform to produce a trustworthy composite answer.
Output includes the best answer, alternative viewpoints from other models, and transparency on which AIs performed correctly with reasoning, enabling informed decision-making.
The approach is particularly valuable in high-stakes domains (healthcare, defense, finance) where single-model hallucination can have serious consequences.
By grounding LLM outputs against ontology data, the Synthesiser bridges the gap between generative AI capabilities and the need for factual accuracy in enterprise settings.

OSDK enables external access to Ontology data via Python, supporting Jupyter Lab notebook integration and local development workflows.
Compute Modules allow bringing external codebases (geocoding services, email integrations, custom servers) into the Foundry platform.
Community examples demonstrate integration patterns between Foundry and external services (Gmail, Google Calendar, ElevenLabs, WLED controllers) through various SDK mechanisms.
A free AIP Developer Account is available for new developers to start building on the platform.

title: Closed or Open Architectured Ontologies: The Enterprise Race to Make Data AI-Ready - Timbr.ai (Medium)

extractedEntities.name: Ontology-Centric Enterprise OS
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's architectural approach where Foundry's ontology abstracts complex technical data into operational objects that mirror how the business operates, embedding governance, lineage, and operational logic directly into the ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Unified semantic foundation that gives meaning and relationships to data, extending beyond a simple semantic layer by connecting disparate sources through shared definitions, relationships, rules, and metrics.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that popularized the concept of an ontology-centric enterprise OS, abstracting complex data into operational objects for real-time decision-making.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Operational abstraction in the ontology representing business entities (aircraft, supply chains, financial entities) that mirror how the business itself operates.

extractedEntities.name: Semantic Representation
extractedEntities.description: The process of creating an ontology that gives semantic context to raw data, enabling LLMs and AI agents to understand entity relationships through a common vocabulary.

extractedEntities.name: Digital Twin of Enterprise
extractedEntities.description: Pattern enabled by ontology-driven architecture where the ontology creates a digital representation of the enterprise, allowing users to analyze, simulate, and act on data through intuitive applications.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes of Object Types that carry semantic meaning within the ontology, enabling machines to reason about data relationships.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships between Object Types in the ontology that define how entities interrelate.

keyInsights: An ontology provides the missing semantic intelligence layer that enables AI systems (including LLMs) to understand context and relationships between entities like 'customer', 'transaction', and 'region'.
Palantir's ontology abstracts complex technical data into operational objects that mirror how the business operates, embedding governance, lineage, and operational logic directly.
The ontology enables a digital twin of the enterprise, allowing analysis, simulation, and action through intuitive applications in real-time decision-making scenarios.
Ontologies leverage inference capabilities through inheritance and transitive properties to transform data into knowledge that is discoverable, explainable, and machine-navigable.

The article frames the enterprise AI landscape as a divide between closed ontology ecosystems (Palantir) that deliver power through control versus open architectures that prioritize flexibility and interoperability.

title: Python (Spark) - AIP - Orchestrators - Palantir Docs

extractedEntities.name: transforms-aip
extractedEntities.description: Deprecated PySpark library that simplified integration of language model APIs into Spark workflows, enabling completions, embeddings, and vision requests using data within Spark DataFrames.

extractedEntities.name: CompletionOrchestrator
extractedEntities.description: API class in transforms-aip for running LLM completions across Spark DataFrames, handling rate limiting, distributed workload across executors, and error handling.

extractedEntities.name: EmbeddingOrchestrator
extractedEntities.description: API class in transforms-aip for running embedding model requests across Spark DataFrames to generate vector representations of text data.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Recommended alternative to transforms-aip for applying LLMs across datasets, specifically through its Use LLM node.

extractedEntities.name: palantir_models
extractedEntities.description: Library providing model input types (like OpenAiGptChatLanguageModelInput) for integrating language models into Foundry transforms.

extractedEntities.name: Rate Limit Management
extractedEntities.description: Feature of transforms-aip that handles both request-based and token-based rate limiting for LLM API calls within Spark jobs.

extractedEntities.name: Distributed LLM Processing
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where LLM workloads are distributed equally across Spark executors to maximize parallelism and optimize request speed within rate limits.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core platform hosting the transforms-aip library and Spark-based data pipelines.

keyInsights: The transforms-aip library is deprecated in favor of the palantir_models library or Pipeline Builder's Use LLM node for applying LLMs across datasets.
The library provided CompletionOrchestrator and EmbeddingOrchestrator classes that distributed LLM workloads across Spark executors with automatic rate limiting.
Error handling captures failure information per request and continues execution to avoid failing the entire build, enabling resilient batch LLM processing.
The KUBERNETES_NO_EXECUTORS profile is recommended for optimum speed and cost efficiency when running transforms-aip workflows.
The library bridges data engineering pipelines (PySpark DataFrames) with AI capabilities (LLM completions and embeddings) using minimal configuration.

title: AIP Logic Semantic Search Block - Palantir Developer Community

extractedEntities.name: AIP Logic
extractedEntities.description: No-code LLM-powered function development environment that includes a semantic search block for finding closest items based on property embeddings.

extractedEntities.name: Property

extractedEntities.name: Embedding Model Configuration
extractedEntities.description: Per-property configuration in the Ontology object type settings that determines which embedding model converts queries and properties for semantic search, supporting both built-in and custom Foundry ML Live Models.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity whose properties can be configured with embedding models for semantic search in AIP Logic.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Executable logic unit in Foundry that can include semantic search capabilities through AIP Logic blocks.

keyInsights: The semantic search block in AIP Logic uses embedding models configured per-property within the Ontology object type configuration, not a global model.
Organizations can use custom Foundry ML Live Models as the embedding model for semantic search, allowing enrollment-wide control over which models are available.
Semantic search capability is tightly integrated with the Ontology's property-level configuration, making it a first-class feature of the object type definition.
The embedding model choice for semantic search is set at the property level in object type configuration, giving granular control over search behavior.

title: Model Context Protocol (MCP) - Palantir Developer Community

extractedEntities.name: Foundry

extractedEntities.name: MCP as Integration Layer
extractedEntities.description: Proposed pattern where Foundry offers MCP as a new integration type (similar to REST) to expose existing MCP server ecosystem functions as Foundry Functions available to Agents and App Builders.

extractedEntities.name: Continue

extractedEntities.name: Function

keyInsights: Community developers have built MCP servers using Palantir's public SDK that enable listing/querying datasets and ontology objects plus listing/executing functions from external AI tools.
A proposed integration pattern would have Foundry expose MCP as a new integration type alongside REST, allowing the entire ecosystem of existing MCP servers to become accessible as Foundry Functions.
MCP integration enables external AI tools (Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code Copilot) to access Foundry data and execute functions, extending the platform beyond its native interfaces.
The Continue module in VS Code supports MCP connectivity to Foundry, though configuration approaches vary between AI tools.

title: AIP Logic - FAQ - Palantir Docs

extractedEntities.name: AIP Logic
extractedEntities.description: No-code AI function development environment where LLM-powered functions are built using blocks, with features for token management, unit testing, deterministic transforms, and semantic search integration.

extractedEntities.name: Use LLM Block
extractedEntities.description: A block type in AIP Logic that invokes an LLM for processing, with its own context window and token limit, and can be split into multiple blocks for intermediate processing steps.

extractedEntities.name: Transform Block
extractedEntities.description: Deterministic block in AIP Logic that performs data transformations without using any tokens, producing more predictable outcomes than LLM blocks.

extractedEntities.name: Execute Block
extractedEntities.description: Deterministic block in AIP Logic for executing operations without consuming tokens.

extractedEntities.name: Apply Action Block
extractedEntities.description: Deterministic block in AIP Logic that applies an action without consuming tokens, used for performing ontology modifications.

extractedEntities.name: Block Splitting Strategy
extractedEntities.description: Performance optimization pattern where single large LLM blocks are split into multiple smaller blocks, each with its own context window, reducing token usage and improving execution speed and consistency.

extractedEntities.name: Token Limit Management
extractedEntities.description: Token limits reset per-block in AIP Logic; the debugger shows token usage with a red bar indicating when reduction is needed for reliable performance.

extractedEntities.name: Query Objects Tool
extractedEntities.description: Tool available within AIP Logic that allows the LLM to query objects, with the recommendation to select a subset of properties to reduce tokens sent to the LLM.

extractedEntities.name: Few-Shot Prompting in Logic
extractedEntities.description: Performance pattern of providing example input/output pairs to the LLM within AIP Logic blocks, significantly enhancing model comprehension and output quality.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: The output of AIP Logic development that can be used across the platform, including in applications, automations, and other integrations.

keyInsights: AIP Logic token limits reset per-block, so splitting large blocks into multiple smaller ones gives each its own context window and reduces the risk of hitting limits.
Deterministic blocks (transform, execute, apply action) use zero tokens and produce more predictable outcomes, making them preferred for operations that do not require LLM reasoning.
Selecting specific properties from input objects and query results is critical for reducing token consumption; the raw view in the debugger shows the actual string sent to the LLM.
Unit tests (5-10 saved input/output pairs) should be run every time prompts are modified to catch regressions in LLM performance.
The LLM temperature and model parameters are configurable to enable fine-tuning of output determinism, and semantic search is supported through Logic integration.
Few-shot examples in the system prompt significantly enhance LLM performance by making tasks more comprehensible.

title: Action types - Parameters - Object dropdown security considerations - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Object Dropdown Security
extractedEntities.description: Security consideration where static value filters in object dropdown validations are exposed to all users who can view the action type, risking exposure of property value combinations to unauthorized users.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology concept representing executable business operations whose parameter dropdowns may expose sensitive property values through static filters.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Object Type attribute whose values can be inadvertently exposed through static value filters in action type dropdown parameters.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity (e.g., Document) whose property values may be revealed through action type dropdown filters.

extractedEntities.name: Static Value Filter
extractedEntities.description: A filter type in object dropdown validations that uses hardcoded values, which gets converted into an object set visible in network requests, potentially revealing property values to unauthorized users.

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: The runtime representation of an object dropdown validation filter, containing the filter criteria including static values that could be inspected in network requests.

extractedEntities.name: Object Visibility
extractedEntities.description: Access control mechanism where users cannot view certain objects, but static value filters in action dropdowns may reveal the existence of specific property values for those objects.

extractedEntities.name: Parameter-Based Filtering
extractedEntities.description: Security mitigation pattern where object dropdown filters use object properties or user-provided parameters instead of static values, avoiding exposure of sensitive data in the action type definition.

keyInsights: Static value filters in action type dropdowns are converted into object sets whose filter criteria can be inspected in network requests, potentially revealing property values to unauthorized users.
The mitigation is to use object properties or user-provided parameters for filtering instead of static values, as parameter-based filters do not expose underlying data.

The actions backend redacts sensitive information (like submission criteria) from the action type definition for non-editors, but dropdown validations become visible as object sets at form-viewing time.
This is a visibility concern rather than a direct data access issue -- users can learn that a property value exists but cannot necessarily view the corresponding objects.

title: Palantir MCP - Security - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Palantir MCP
extractedEntities.description: Model Context Protocol integration providing secure connection between external AI systems and Foundry resources, with different security models for platform-based and local environment usage.

extractedEntities.name: MCP Data Governance
extractedEntities.description: Security framework for MCP that varies based on deployment context: platform usage inherits Foundry's security model with data remaining in-environment, while local usage sends tool outputs to third-party LLM providers.

extractedEntities.name: MCP Write Access Controls
extractedEntities.description: Security restrictions on MCP write operations where LLM agents can create new datasets but cannot update or delete existing ones, and all ontology modifications require human approval through proposal review.

extractedEntities.name: Proposal Review
extractedEntities.description: Required human approval process for all ontology modifications made through MCP, ensuring that changes must be reviewed and merged by a human before affecting the main ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Core platform whose security model is inherited by MCP connections, with Control Panel used to enable/disable MCP for local development.

extractedEntities.name: Control Panel
extractedEntities.description: Foundry administration interface used to enable Palantir MCP for local development, which is disabled by default.

extractedEntities.name: Continue
extractedEntities.description: VS Code extension for using Palantir MCP within the Foundry platform with Palantir-provided third-party LLMs.

extractedEntities.name: Data Flow Security Model
extractedEntities.description: Differentiated security model where platform-based MCP usage keeps data within the Foundry environment, while local MCP usage sends tool outputs to external LLM providers (Anthropic for Claude Code, Microsoft for VS Code Copilot).

keyInsights: Palantir MCP for local development is disabled by default and must be explicitly enabled in Control Panel, reflecting a secure-by-default posture.

Data governance differs dramatically between platform and local MCP usage: platform usage keeps data within Foundry, while local usage sends tool outputs to respective LLM providers (Anthropic, Microsoft, etc.).
MCP write tools are intentionally limited: agents can create datasets but not update/delete existing ones, and all ontology modifications require human-approved proposal reviews.
The security model ensures non-destructive operations by default, with human-in-the-loop approval required for any ontology changes made through MCP-connected AI tools.
Continue in VS Code within Foundry uses Palantir-provided third-party LLMs, keeping data within the organization's Foundry environment.

title: Download controls - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Download Controls
extractedEntities.description: Foundry security capabilities that limit users' ability to download/export data from the platform, providing defense-in-depth against unauthorized data transfer when combined with other strategies.

extractedEntities.name: Checkpoints
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism that requires user acknowledgement or justification before proceeding with a download, reducing unintentional data downloads and creating audit records.

extractedEntities.name: Discoverer Role
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry role that does not include the ability to perform download operations on a specific resource, serving as a first line of defense for download control.

extractedEntities.name: Custom Roles
extractedEntities.description: User-defined roles in Foundry that can remove download-related workflows while preserving more privileged operations on a resource.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core platform providing multiple download control mechanisms including roles, checkpoints, and audit logging.

extractedEntities.name: Defense in Depth for Downloads
extractedEntities.description: Security pattern combining roles, checkpoints, and audit logs to provide layered control over download actions, acknowledging that no single mechanism is comprehensive against all data transfer methods.

extractedEntities.name: Download Definition
extractedEntities.description: In Foundry, a download is an action transferring data to a user's local machine, typically via Export/Download buttons in the UI (CSV export, Contour export, Workshop export to Excel).

extractedEntities.name: Shared Security Responsibility Model
extractedEntities.description: Security model where customers maintain responsibility over their data, access controls, and usage policies, with Foundry providing the technical mechanisms to enforce them.

keyInsights: Download controls alone are not comprehensive; clipboard copying, screenshots, automated exports, webhooks, and external system calls are all alternative data transfer vectors that require separate controls.
Foundry provides layered download defense: roles (Discoverer role or custom roles), checkpoints (user acknowledgement/justification), and audit logs for monitoring.
The Discoverer role specifically excludes download operations, while custom roles can selectively remove download workflows while preserving other privileged operations.
Checkpoints reduce unintentional downloads by requiring acknowledgement or justification before proceeding, creating accountability records.
Foundry operates under a shared security responsibility model where customers own responsibility for data governance while the platform provides enforcement mechanisms.

title: Protecting your self-hosted Foundry installation - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Self-Hosted Security
extractedEntities.description: Comprehensive security guidance for Foundry installations deployed outside Palantir's managed SaaS platform, covering physical access, encryption, identity security, network security, and zero trust architecture.

extractedEntities.name: Data at Rest Encryption
extractedEntities.description: Security requirement for self-hosted Foundry where all underlying servers and storage devices must use full-disk encryption (LUKS, self-encrypting drives, or cloud provider solutions) with AES-256 or AES-128, in addition to Foundry's application-level encryption.

extractedEntities.name: Data in Transit Encryption
extractedEntities.description: Security requirement mandating TLS 1.2+ for all client-Foundry communications, with strong cipher suites (ECDHE with CHACHA-POLY1305 or AES-GCM), avoiding CBC-mode ciphers where possible.

extractedEntities.name: HSM Key Management
extractedEntities.description: Recommendation to store cryptographic key material in hardware security modules for self-hosted Foundry installations.

extractedEntities.name: Zero Trust Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Recommended security posture for self-hosted Foundry using modern zero trust technologies, where no implicit trust is granted based on network location.

extractedEntities.name: Identity Security
extractedEntities.description: Requirements for self-hosted Foundry including centralized SSO, named accounts (no sharing), strong multi-factor authentication (preferring FIDO2), and passwordless authentication.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core platform designed for secure collaboration that can be deployed self-hosted in customer datacenters or clouds.

extractedEntities.name: Apollo
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's deployment management system referenced in the context of managing self-hosted Foundry installations.

keyInsights: Self-hosted Foundry installations require defense in depth: physical security, full-disk encryption (AES-256/128), application-level encryption, and TLS 1.2+ with strong cipher suites.
HSM-backed key management is recommended for cryptographic material, and ECDHE with CHACHA-POLY1305 or AES-GCM is the recommended cipher suite configuration.
Identity security mandates centralized SSO, named accounts (no sharing), and strong MFA preferring FIDO2, with SMS/email-based MFA explicitly discouraged.
Zero trust architecture is recommended where no implicit trust is granted based on network position, complementing traditional perimeter-based security.
Foundry provides application-level encryption at rest by default, but self-hosted deployments must additionally encrypt underlying storage and enforce full-disk encryption.

title: Why you should govern data access through Purpose-Based Access Control - Dataminded on Medium

extractedEntities.name: Purpose-Based Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Data access governance methodology where access is granted to purposes (applications of data) rather than individuals, allowing users to work within the context of a single purpose while potentially being assigned to multiple purposes.

extractedEntities.name: RBAC
extractedEntities.description: Role-Based Access Control, a technical implementation for organizing permission sets that can be combined with PBAC by scoping purpose-based permissions to roles like data scientist or data engineer.

extractedEntities.name: ABAC
extractedEntities.description: Attribute-Based Access Control, a technical permission mechanism that can be combined with PBAC at the purpose level.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's governance platform referenced as an implementation of purpose-based access controls for data governance.

extractedEntities.name: Purpose-Scoped Data Access
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where all data access occurs within the context of a single purpose, ensuring that cross-referencing of data across purposes requires explicit approval and maintaining GDPR-aligned purpose limitation.

extractedEntities.name: Data Access Governance
extractedEntities.description: The component of data governance concerned with controlling who has access to what data, serving as a defense against cyberattacks, data leakage, and enabling regulatory compliance.

keyInsights: PBAC grants access to purposes (applications of data) rather than individuals, with users always accessing data within the context of a single purpose even if assigned to multiple purposes.
PBAC operates at a different level than RBAC/ABAC and can be combined with them; RBAC/ABAC define permission sets while PBAC assigns those sets to purposes instead of individuals.

Benefits include flexible team onboarding/offboarding (all purpose access is bundled), consistent access across team members in the same role, and GDPR-aligned purpose limitation and data minimization.
PBAC makes cross-referencing of data across purposes require explicit approval, preventing unauthorized data combination and maintaining proportionality principles.
Palantir Foundry is cited as a governance tool implementing purpose-based access controls, demonstrating the concept's enterprise applicability.
